Transcriptomic & single-cell translational analyses on gynecologic cancer patients receiving MEK-inhibitors in the FINPROVE trial

Transcriptomic & single-cell translational analyses on gynecologic cancer patients receiving MEK-inhibitors in the FINPROVE trial

Background

Background

FINPROVE is Finland’s first multi-center, non-randomized phase II clinical trial advancing precision cancer medicine. It evaluates the efficacy of EMA/FDA-approved targeted therapies based on tumor molecular profiles in patients who have exhausted standard treatments. Despite shared genomic alterations, treatment responses in gynecological cancers vary, highlighting the need for deeper molecular insight.

FINPROVE is Finland’s first multi-center, non-randomized phase II clinical trial advancing precision cancer medicine. It evaluates the efficacy of EMA/FDA-approved targeted therapies based on tumor molecular profiles in patients who have exhausted standard treatments. Despite shared genomic alterations, treatment responses in gynecological cancers vary, highlighting the need for deeper molecular insight.

Methods

Methods

Between 1/2022 and 4/2025, tumors of 765 patients underwent targeted next-generation sequencing (NGS), of which 103 (13%) were gynecological cancers. NGS data were analyzed to identify potentially actionable alterations. A subset of tumors underwent further characterization using NanoString gene expression profiling and single-cell spatial proteomics. To date, 17 samples have been analyzed with tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (tCycIF) and 12 with NanoString nCounter profiling, aiming for a cohort of at least 36 patients.

Between 1/2022 and 4/2025, tumors of 765 patients underwent targeted next-generation sequencing (NGS), of which 103 (13%) were gynecological cancers. NGS data were analyzed to identify potentially actionable alterations. A subset of tumors underwent further characterization using NanoString gene expression profiling and single-cell spatial proteomics. To date, 17 samples have been analyzed with tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (tCycIF) and 12 with NanoString nCounter profiling, aiming for a cohort of at least 36 patients.

Results

Results

The most frequent gynecological cancers were high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC, n=28), low-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (LGSC, n=14) and uterine leiomyosarcoma (n=9). The most frequent targetable alterations were in KRAS (n=18) and BRAF (n=3). At data cutoff, 28 patients had received targeted treatment with a median treatment duration of 4 months. Among them, 1 complete response, 9 partial responses, 8 stable diseases, and 10 progressions were observed resulting in a disease control rate of 64% at 16 weeks, the trial’s primary endpoint. Preliminary NanoString data revealed differential MAPK pathway gene expression correlating with treatment responses. Moreover, expression of interferon-related genes correlated with response duration. The tCycIF analyses revealed distinct spatial MAPK pathway activation patterns across histologies, with a prominent activation on the tumor boundaries in LGSC.

The most frequent gynecological cancers were high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC, n=28), low-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (LGSC, n=14) and uterine leiomyosarcoma (n=9). The most frequent targetable alterations were in KRAS (n=18) and BRAF (n=3). At data cutoff, 28 patients had received targeted treatment with a median treatment duration of 4 months. Among them, 1 complete response, 9 partial responses, 8 stable diseases, and 10 progressions were observed resulting in a disease control rate of 64% at 16 weeks, the trial’s primary endpoint. Preliminary NanoString data revealed differential MAPK pathway gene expression correlating with treatment responses. Moreover, expression of interferon-related genes correlated with response duration. The tCycIF analyses revealed distinct spatial MAPK pathway activation patterns across histologies, with a prominent activation on the tumor boundaries in LGSC.

Conclusions

Conclusions

Frequent KRAS and BRAF alterations were identified in gynecological cancers, with a subset of patients showing clinical benefit from MEK inhibitors correlating with MAPK and interferon-related gene expressions. Spatial analysis revealed MAPK pathway activation localized to tumor boundaries, particularly in LGSC, underscoring the influence of the tumor microenvironment on treatment response.

Frequent KRAS and BRAF alterations were identified in gynecological cancers, with a subset of patients showing clinical benefit from MEK inhibitors correlating with MAPK and interferon-related gene expressions. Spatial analysis revealed MAPK pathway activation localized to tumor boundaries, particularly in LGSC, underscoring the influence of the tumor microenvironment on treatment response.

Funding

Funding

Roche Oy, Novartis Oy, Bayer Oy, Lilly Oy, Janssen, Merck and Incyte have provided seventeen drugs as part of the clinical study. Pfizer Oy has been supporting the FINPROVE Molecular Tumor Board (MTB) development. Cancer Foundation Finland has financially supported the FINPROVE trial. Eschner Foundation has financially supported patient prescreening at Turku University Hospital.
